content:
Ten rules ranked by blast radius, the session loop, a worked example, and the commands that recover a session gone wrong.

13 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Picture a junior developer at the next desk — fast, tireless, with zero memory of yesterday. You wouldn’t hand that person a one-line ticket and walk away. You’d have a conversation: context in, output reviewed, corrections given, direction held.
That conversation, run at high speed, is what vibe coding actually is. Not a shortcut around engineering judgment — a prompt loop that moves fast enough that bad judgment compounds in minutes instead of sprints. Everything below is the discipline that keeps that speed from working against you.
What vibe coding actually is
Vibe coding is prompting an agent casually and accepting output on a “does it seem to work?” check — fine for prototypes and throwaway exploration, risky past that. I’ve written separately about where this mode ends and production-grade agentic engineering begins; this piece assumes that split and covers the practices that keep the casual mode from turning into expensive rework.
The mindset
Two consequences follow from the junior-developer framing. Your role shifts from writing code to directing it — you set the vision, review what comes back, keep the project pointed the right way. And the floor doesn’t disappear: you still need programming basics to review output and catch nonsense. What you no longer need is syntax memorized or every detail worked out by hand.
That floor has a real shape. At minimum:

• Git — branches, commits, reverting. Every recovery habit below assumes you already have this muscle.

• Hosting — Vercel, Netlify, a VPS — so shipped means live for someone else, not just running on your machine.

• Basic CLI — navigating directories, running a script, reading an error instead of panicking at it.

• SSH — the secure remote shell that gets you onto a server that isn’t your laptop.

• What frameworks actually do — React versus a backend framework versus a meta-framework — so a stack choice is a judgment call, not a guess.

• What an API is — a contract two programs talk over, which is most of what an agent is wiring together when it “adds a feature.”

• Networking basics — what a port, a domain, and HTTPS actually are.
None of it needs mastery. Just enough to read what the agent hands back instead of trusting it on faith.
Plan before you prompt
The cheapest fixes happen before the first prompt. Four decisions, in order:
Define the MVP, phase the rest. Start with the simplest version that works — for a habit tracker, that’s add a habit, check in, see a streak, nothing else. Accounts are phase two, reminders phase three. You always have something working, and an agent performs measurably better on small focused asks than on “build the whole app.”
Pick a popular stack. React, Next.js, Tailwind, Supabase, Python — an agent has seen enormous amounts of code in these, so output quality is higher and error rates lower. Boring is a feature here. Once you’ve picked one, prefer its official generator over agent-written boilerplate — npm create vite@latest, npx create-next-app, rails new - and let the agent build on top of the scaffold it creates. The generator tracks the framework's current conventions; an agent's training data is a snapshot that's already stale the day a major version ships.
Write a short spec. One document: features, user flows, data shapes, and any rules that must hold. Hand it to the agent at the start of each session so it always has the full picture instead of reconstructing your intent from fragments. The spec is the durable artifact; the sessions are disposable.
Keep the always-on config lean. The spec is per-session context; the config file (CLAUDE.md or equivalent) loads on every turn, so it gets the opposite treatment - roughly 60 lines, only what the agent can't infer. npx leanharness scaffolds that file, plus the deny rules and skills that back it, in one command. I wrote more on setting up a minimal Claude Code harness and on why agents ignore a bloated CLAUDE.md in the first place.
For the fuller method behind the MVP-and-phase call — measurable goals over adjectives, risk-first task order, a checkpoint that catches sunk cost before it wastes a session — see how to plan and scope a build with an LLM.
The session loop
The shape of a good session doesn’t change with the tool. Pick the right one for the job, then run this loop per feature.
Initialize the project. git init before the agent touches anything - every accepted change should be a commit you can inspect or revert, not an untracked mutation. Run the stack's scaffolding CLI here too, before the first prompt.
Write a lean config file, per the planning step above, then open Plan Mode before writing a line — let the agent lay out its approach and the files it intends to touch, and read that plan before any code changes.
Then write the first prompt as a scope, not a wish — one goal, an explicit list of what to touch and what not to, and a condition that tells you when it’s done:
Goal: <one feature, one sentence>
Touch only: <files or directories>
Do not touch: src/auth/**, src/billing/**, migrations/**
Done when: <tests and typecheck pass, or a specific behavior is observable>That last line matters beyond this one prompt. An agent given a fuzzy goal reports success on a fuzzy basis; tying “done” to something it can check itself is the same discipline a verifiable completion condition demands of any autonomous loop.
From there: review the diff before accepting it — scan specifically for file deletions, changed API signatures, new dependencies, and schema edits, the four changes most likely to be silently wrong. Run tests, typecheck, and lint immediately, not at the end of the session — a failure traced to the last diff takes seconds to fix, one traced to an hour of accepted changes takes an investigation. Commit on green, then /clear context before the next feature - carryover from a finished feature becomes a false assumption on the next one. Know the recovery commands before you need them. And deploy only once it's actually done, not once it compiles - a working build and a done feature aren't the same claim.
Worked example: five prompts, one small tool
Concrete beats abstract. Here’s the loop applied to a habit tracker — a CLI that logs a daily check-in per habit and reports a streak.
Prompt 1 — scaffold, nothing else. Scaffold a Node.js/TypeScript CLI habit tracker: package.json, tsconfig.json, and an index.ts that parses process.argv and routes to command handlers - add, check, streak - storing everything in a local habits.json. Route the commands, but don't implement the handlers yet. Scoping out the handlers on the first prompt keeps the diff small enough to actually read.
Prompt 2 — one handler. Implement add <habit-name>: append a new habit to habits.json with name, createdAt, and an empty checkins array, creating the file first if it doesn't exist. Test it immediately: npx ts-node index.ts add "read".
Prompt 3 — the next handler, same shape. Implement check <habit-name>, which appends today's ISO date to that habit's checkins array if it isn't already there today. Test: npx ts-node index.ts check "read".
Prompt 4 — the payoff feature. Implement streak <habit-name>, which counts consecutive days ending today with a check-in and prints the count - with a helpful error instead of a crash if the habit doesn't exist.
Prompt 5 — the edge cases a demo always skips. Add input validation: add and check without a name print correct usage instead of crashing; an unknown command prints the three valid ones. This is the prompt most vibe-coding sessions skip because the happy path already "worked" - it's also the one guardrail (run tests after every accepted change, below) exists to force.
Four prompts to working, a fifth to not embarrassing. That ratio holds for most small tools built this way.
Ten rules, ranked by what breaks if you skip them
They aren’t equally urgent. Skip the first cluster and a session can damage something real. Skip the second and it degrades turn over turn. Skip the third and you’re redoing work you’d already saved.
Guardrails — protect what hurts to get wrong:

• Name the directories an agent may never touch without an explicit prompt — auth, billing, migrations — in the config file, and repeat the list in any prompt that touches adjacent code.

• Review every diff before accepting it.

• Run tests, typecheck, and lint after every accepted change — catching a bad change one diff late is routine, catching it five diffs late is archaeology.

• Require a written migration plan before any schema change ships — forward SQL, a rollback path, a verification step. Schema is the one category of edit a git revert doesn't cleanly undo once data has landed on the new shape.
Context hygiene — what loads every turn should earn its keep:

• Keep the config file lean — roughly 60 lines, only what the agent can’t infer. Claude Code’s own system prompt already spends about 50 directives before yours even loads, and reliability drops off past roughly 150–200 total — a bloated file gets discounted wholesale, including the parts that mattered. Lean isn’t the same as frozen: append a decision that will still matter next month, prune what stopped being true.

• Reset context between features with /clear.

• Open Plan Mode before every new feature — reviewing the proposed approach before a line changes catches a misread requirement while it’s a one-line correction, not a diff to unwind.
Reuse — build the pattern once, invoke it every time:

• Save the scoped prompt (goal / touch / done-when) as a paste-in snippet, not something you retype from memory, where details quietly drop.

• Hand over an example file instead of describing your style — “match this file” resolves in one shot what a paragraph of stylistic adjectives won’t.

• Codify recurring prompts into reusable skills — test generation, security review, safe refactors — each written once and invoked consistently instead of re-explained per session. Check the ecosystem first; an existing skill usually beats a first draft. More on composing several skills together.
Git is the safety net
Version control does more work in a vibe-coding loop than in hand-written code, because the agent can change a lot of files very fast — sometimes wrongly.
Start every feature from a clean slate — commit or stash before the agent starts something new, so a sideways feature costs you nothing to discard. Commit after every task that lands green; small commits are the checkpoint system that makes the whole loop safe. Revert with git, not the tool’s own undo — /rewind is convenient mid-turn, but across a multi-file change git is the only rollback that returns you to an exact, known state. And let the agent handle the git chores - commit messages, branch creation, push mechanics are all delegable; the decisions about what to revert and when to commit stay yours.
Prompt patterns that work
How you phrase requests determines most of what you get back.

• One task per prompt. Stack five requests into one message and errors pile up across all five at once, with no way to tell which instruction caused which failure.

• Be specific. Layout, behavior, content, constraints — a vague prompt produces a vague result, and the correction round costs more than the specificity would have.

• Show, don’t describe. Paste existing files, attach mockups or screenshots, link references — material the agent can look at beats prose it has to interpret.

• State the outcome, not the mistake. Keep a running list of the agent’s repeat offenses and turn each into a positive instruction — “use the real API response for every field,” not “don’t add placeholder data.” A negative instruction only rules out one specific miss; naming the outcome you want closes the gap a negation leaves open.

• Use “act as” framing when you want
